diagnostic_question: Given the exact per-request history-response score deltas already produced by the Transformer, does their real candidate attribution outperform random attribution, and how much ranking headroom appears if the unchanged delta multiset is assigned in label-aligned order?

intervention.actual: Use each candidate's observed true-history minus null-history score delta.
intervention.random: Randomly permute the same delta multiset across the fixed candidate slate 128 times per request.
intervention.gain_aligned: Monotonically assign the unchanged delta multiset by observed gain; this is a label-oracle diagnostic, not a deployable method.
invariants: same number and exact multiset of delta values per request

valid_inference: On Lite strict-nonrepeat requests, the Transformer creates large candidate-relative score changes but their observed allocation is not reliably better than random allocation of the same changes.

The same response-value multiset has substantial ranking headroom when its candidate attribution is label-aligned, localizing the observed loss to direction/allocation rather than absence of response magnitude.
Repeat requests are a positive control: their observed attribution is reliably better than random and converts more of the available direction headroom.
invalid_inference: The label-aligned condition is not a trainable or deployable result.
Oracle headroom alone does not prove that strict-nonrepeat direction is recoverable from available train information.
One encoder family, objective, seed and Lite dataset do not establish a universal Transformer limitation or predict the Full result.
